preamble: The shift from the bilabial nasal [m] to the alveolar stop [d] involves two articulatory changes: denasalization (raising the velum) and a shift in the place of articulation from the lips to the alveolar ridge. While rare as a direct historical law, it is a hallmark of certain language families undergoing systematic nasal-to-stop shifts.

examples.note: Historical denasalization where nasals became voiced stops; in some contexts, the place of articulation also shifted during systemic reorganization.
Developmental substitution where the child replaces the nasal with a stop and shifts the articulation to the central alveolar position.
